pattern: With at most four pieces, enumerating every piece's candidate destinations and backtracking through the small Cartesian product of choices while simulating second-by-second occupancy finds every collision-free combination.
hints: For each piece, list every square it could travel toward in a straight line (including staying put) as a (direction, distance) pair.
A move combination is one candidate per piece; because there are at most four pieces, you can afford to try every combination.

For a chosen combination, replay time second by second: a piece's position at time t is its start plus direction times min(t, its distance), and the combination is invalid the instant two pieces share a square at the same second.

edge_inputs.description: single rook away from the corner
single bishop pinned to a corner, only one diagonal available
four pieces filling the maximum piece count with no queen
two rooks directly adjacent on the same row, forcing many near-miss collisions
two bishops on the same diagonal one step apart
a queen and a rook sharing a row from opposite ends
three mixed pieces including the only queen allowed on the board

stress.description: random legal piece sets (at most one queen) at random distinct squares
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 4)
    types = ['rook', 'bishop']
    kinds = []
    if rng.random() < 0.5:
        kinds.append('queen')
    while len(kinds) < n:
        kinds.append(rng.choice(types))
    rng.shuffle(kinds)
    all_cells = [(r, c) for r in range(1, 9) for c in range(1, 9)]
    rng.shuffle(all_cells)
    positions = [[r, c] for r, c in all_cells[:n]]
    return [kinds, positions]
stress.note: state space is inherently bounded by n<=4 pieces on an 8x8 board, so 'size' just seeds variety, not scale
